summary: DHH argues that corporate mission statements are not just useless but actively harmful. He contends that typical mission statements contain no real trade-offs or guidance, making them meaningless platitudes about customer satisfaction and excellence. Worse, they create impossible ideals that set companies up to fail by turning every normal shortcoming into a perceived betrayal. Happy customers see the mission as merely meeting the stated bar, while unhappy ones feel personally deceived. The only mission statement worth printing would be one that actually said something contrarian.
key_points: Mission statements that involve no trade-offs offer no actual guidance for decision-making
Lofty promises create a no-win scenario: happy customers see it as baseline expectation, unhappy ones feel betrayed
Corporate mission statements create impossible ideals disconnected from everyday business reality
Every normal business failing gets reframed as a transgression against the company's stated mission
money_quotes: Chances are far better that the rainbow springs straight out of a dumpster than a pot of gold, and that the mission statement is trash too.
These mission statements – that never contain neither a real mission nor any interesting statements – involve no trade-offs, and thus offer no guidance.
A mission statement that actually said "fuck the customers, we strive for mediocrity, and this is all about the short term"? Now that would be worth printing.
Waxing lyrically about all the happy customers, the happy employees, and all the other win-win bullshit is placing a bet you can only lose. Zero upside, unlimited downside.
When customers invariably are disgruntled, they'll point to your saccharine mission statement and not just feel aggrieved, but betrayed.
It creates an impossible ideal crafted in an abstract world that has no bearing on reality.
key_insight: Mission statements that express no trade-offs provide no guidance, and by setting impossible ideals they guarantee that every normal failing feels like a betrayal rather than just business as usual.
